title: JavaScript
description: Learn browser JavaScript from variables and functions to arrays, objects, the DOM, and events.

lessons.title: JS Introduction
lessons.subtitle: What JavaScript is, where it runs, and your first script
lessons.theory:
JavaScript is a versatile programming language that runs in web browsers, on servers (Node.js), and in many other environments. In the browser, it makes pages interactive: validating forms, updating content without reloads, and responding to user input.

HTML provides structure, CSS provides style, and JavaScript provides behavior. Scripts can live in external .js files (recommended) or inline in HTML. Place deferred scripts at the end of <body> or use defer so the DOM is ready before code runs.

The console in DevTools is your first debugging tool. console.log() prints values; errors appear in red with file names and line numbers. Modern JavaScript uses ES2015+ features supported by all current browsers.

JavaScript is dynamically typed: variables can hold different types over time. It uses curly braces for blocks and semicolons are optional but often used for clarity. Learning the fundamentals here prepares you for frameworks like React later.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: JavaScript is a high-level scripting language used primarily to add interactivity and logic to web pages in the browser.

keyPoints: JavaScript adds behavior to HTML and CSS
External scripts improve organization and caching
console.log helps inspect values while learning
ES6+ syntax is standard in modern browsers
DevTools Console shows errors and output

quiz.question: What is JavaScript's main role in a typical web page?
quiz.explanation: JavaScript handles behavior and interactivity. HTML structures; CSS styles.

quiz.question: Which method prints a message to the browser console?
quiz.explanation: console.log() is the standard way to debug output in DevTools.

quiz.question: Where should deferred scripts ideally run relative to the DOM?
options: After the document is parsed or with defer
quiz.explanation: defer or placing scripts at the end avoids running before elements exist.

lessons.title: Variables
lessons.subtitle: let, const, naming rules, and reassignment
lessons.theory:
Variables store values you reuse and update. declare with let or const. const prevents reassignment of the binding; let allows it. Avoid var in modern code—it has confusing scope rules.

Names must start with a letter, $, or _, and can contain digits afterward. Use camelCase for variables and functions: lessonCount, userName. Names should describe intent, not single letters (except short loop counters).

You can declare without assigning: let score; then score = 10. const must be initialized when declared. const objects can still have properties changed; only the variable binding is fixed.

Block scope means let and const exist only inside the nearest { } block—loops, if branches, and functions. This prevents accidental leaks compared to older var behavior.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A variable is a named container that holds a value and can be declared with let (reassignable) or const (binding not reassigned).

keyPoints: Prefer const unless you need to reassign
let allows reassignment; const does not
camelCase is the common naming style
Variables are block-scoped with let and const
const still allows mutating object properties

codeExample.label: let and const

quiz.question: Which keyword should you use for a value that never gets reassigned?
quiz.explanation: const prevents reassigning the variable to a new value.

quiz.question: What happens if you reassign a let variable?
quiz.explanation: let is designed for values that change over time.

quiz.question: Which variable name follows common JavaScript style?
quiz.explanation: camelCase (lessonCount) is standard; hyphens are invalid in identifiers.

lessons.title: Data Types
lessons.subtitle: Primitives, typeof, strings, numbers, booleans, and null
lessons.theory:
JavaScript values belong to types. Primitives include string, number, boolean, undefined, null, bigint, and symbol. Objects (including arrays and functions) are reference types.

typeof operator returns a string describing a primitive: typeof 'hi' is 'string', typeof 42 is 'number'. typeof null is 'object'—a long-standing quirk you should remember.

Strings use quotes; template literals use backticks for interpolation: `Hello, ${name}`. Numbers include integers and floats; special values NaN and Infinity exist. Booleans are true and false, often from comparisons.

== loose equality coerces types; === strict equality compares without coercion. Prefer === and !== to avoid surprises like 0 == false being true. undefined means a variable was declared but not assigned; null is an intentional empty value.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Data types classify values in JavaScript as primitives (immutable single values) or objects (collections and complex data).

keyPoints: Primitives: string, number, boolean, undefined, null, bigint, symbol
typeof checks primitive types (with null quirk)
Template literals embed expressions with ${}
Use === for strict equality comparisons
null is assigned intentionally; undefined is the default missing value

codeExample.label: Types and strict equality

quiz.question: What does typeof null return in JavaScript?
quiz.explanation: typeof null returns 'object' due to a historical bug kept for compatibility.

quiz.question: Which operator avoids type coercion in comparisons?
quiz.explanation: === compares value and type without converting operands.

quiz.question: How do you embed a variable in a template string?
quiz.explanation: Backticks enable ${expression} interpolation in template literals.

lessons.title: Functions
lessons.subtitle: Declarations, expressions, arrow functions, and parameters
lessons.theory:
Functions package reusable logic. Function declarations are hoisted: function greet() { } can be called before its line in the file. Function expressions assign a function to a variable and are not hoisted the same way.

Arrow functions (const add = (a, b) => a + b) have concise syntax and inherit this from the surrounding scope—useful in callbacks. For multi-line bodies, use braces and return.

Parameters can have default values: function createUser(name = 'Guest'). Extra arguments passed at call time are ignored unless you use rest syntax (...args).

return sends a value back; functions without return yield undefined. Pure functions that only depend on inputs are easier to test and reason about than functions that mutate global state.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A function is a callable block of code that accepts inputs (parameters) and may return a value to the caller.

keyPoints: Declarations are hoisted; expressions are not
Arrow functions use => and concise syntax
Default parameters apply when arguments are undefined
return exits the function with a value
Keep functions focused on one clear task

codeExample.label: Functions and arrows

quiz.question: What does an arrow function without curly braces return?
options: The value of the expression after =>
quiz.explanation: Concise arrows implicitly return the expression: n => n * 2 returns the product.

quiz.question: How do default parameters work?
options: They run only in strict mode
They apply when the argument is undefined
quiz.explanation: Defaults fill in when the corresponding argument is missing or undefined.

quiz.question: What is returned if a function has no return statement?
quiz.explanation: Functions without return complete with the value undefined.

lessons.title: Arrays
lessons.subtitle: Creating, indexing, and transforming lists of values
lessons.theory:
Arrays store ordered lists: const nums = [1, 2, 3]. Index from 0; use length for the count. Push and pop modify the end; unshift and shift modify the front.

Higher-order methods are essential: map transforms each item, filter keeps items that pass a test, find returns the first match, reduce accumulates a single result. They avoid manual for-loop boilerplate and bugs.

Spread syntax copies or merges arrays: [...a, ...b]. Destructuring pulls items out: const [first, second] = colors. includes() checks membership; indexOf() finds an index.

Arrays can hold mixed types but usually represent homogeneous collections. JSON APIs often return arrays of objects you map into UI components.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: An array is an ordered, zero-indexed collection of values with a length property and many built-in iteration methods.

keyPoints: Indexes start at 0; length is the item count
map, filter, and reduce transform data declaratively
push/pop modify the end of the array
Spread [...arr] copies elements into a new array
includes checks if a value exists

codeExample.label: Array methods

quiz.question: What index is the first element of an array?
quiz.explanation: JavaScript arrays are zero-indexed; the first item is at index 0.

quiz.question: Which method creates a new array with only items that pass a test?
quiz.explanation: filter returns a new array containing elements where the callback returns true.

quiz.question: What does reduce with an initial value 0 typically do for numbers?
options: Accumulates a single combined result like a sum
quiz.explanation: reduce walks the array and combines values into one accumulator result.

lessons.title: Objects
lessons.subtitle: Properties, methods, destructuring, and JSON
lessons.theory:
Objects group related data as key–value pairs: const user = { name: 'Ada', role: 'admin' }. Access with dot notation user.name or brackets user['role'] when the key is dynamic.

Methods are functions stored as properties: const app = { start() { console.log('go'); } }. Inside methods, this refers to the object (behavior varies with arrows—avoid arrows for object methods if you need dynamic this).

Destructuring extracts fields: const { name, role } = user. Spread copies properties: { ...defaults, ...overrides }. Object.keys(obj) lists property names.

JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a text format for APIs. JSON.stringify converts objects to strings; JSON.parse reads strings back. JSON keys must be double-quoted strings.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: An object is a collection of named properties, each holding a value, used to model real-world entities in code.

keyPoints: Curly braces { } create object literals
Dot and bracket notation access properties
Methods are functions as object properties
Destructuring unpacks properties into variables
JSON.stringify and JSON.parse exchange data with APIs

codeExample.label: Object and JSON

quiz.question: How do you access a property named role on object user?
quiz.explanation: Dot notation and bracket notation are the standard access patterns.

quiz.question: What does JSON.stringify do?
options: Parses a string into an object
Converts a value to a JSON string
quiz.explanation: JSON.stringify serializes JavaScript values into a JSON-formatted string.

quiz.question: What does const { title } = lesson accomplish?
quiz.explanation: Destructuring assigns lesson.title to the variable title.

lessons.title: DOM Manipulation
lessons.subtitle: Selecting elements, changing content, classes, and attributes
lessons.theory:
The DOM (Document Object Model) is the browser's tree representation of HTML. JavaScript can read and update it, which changes what users see without a full page reload.

querySelector and querySelectorAll find elements with CSS selectors: document.querySelector('#title'). textContent changes text; innerHTML parses HTML (use carefully to avoid XSS).

classList.add, remove, and toggle switch CSS classes—preferred over changing inline styles for maintainability. setAttribute and dataset (data-* attributes) store custom data on elements.

createElement and append let you build nodes in memory before inserting them. Document fragments batch inserts efficiently. Always check that selectors match an element before using it to avoid null errors.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: DOM manipulation is updating the live HTML tree in the browser using JavaScript to change content, structure, or attributes.

keyPoints: document.querySelector uses CSS selectors
textContent sets plain text; innerHTML parses markup
classList toggles CSS classes safely
createElement builds new nodes programmatically
Guard against null when an element is missing

codeExample.label: Update the DOM

quiz.question: Which API selects the first element matching a CSS selector?
quiz.explanation: querySelector returns the first match for any valid CSS selector.

quiz.question: Why is classList.toggle often better than editing style directly?
options: It keeps presentation in CSS classes
quiz.explanation: Classes separate styling in CSS from behavior in JavaScript.

quiz.question: What does element.dataset.status refer to?
quiz.explanation: dataset maps to data-* attributes: data-status becomes dataset.status.

lessons.title: Events
lessons.subtitle: Listeners, the event object, propagation, and forms
lessons.theory:
Browsers emit events when users interact: clicks, keyboard input, form submission, and more. addEventListener attaches a handler: button.addEventListener('click', handler). Named functions or arrows both work; removeListener needs the same function reference.

The event object describes what happened: event.target is the element that triggered the event; event.preventDefault() stops default behavior like form navigation; stopPropagation limits bubbling up the tree.

Event delegation attaches one listener on a parent and checks event.target—efficient for lists that change. Input events fire on every keystroke; change often fires when a control loses focus after editing.

Keyboard accessibility matters: support Enter and Space on custom buttons, and do not rely on click alone for critical actions. Passive listeners improve scroll performance for touch and wheel events.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: An event is a signal that something occurred in the browser; a listener runs your callback when that event fires on an element.

keyPoints: addEventListener registers handlers without overwriting HTML
event.target identifies the element that triggered the event
preventDefault stops default browser actions
Delegation handles dynamic child elements efficiently
Input and change events suit forms and controls

codeExample.label: Form and delegation

quiz.question: How do you stop a form from reloading the page on submit?
quiz.explanation: preventDefault() cancels the default submit navigation so you handle data in JS.

quiz.question: What is event delegation?
options: Attaching many listeners to every child
Listening on a parent and handling events from children
quiz.explanation: One parent listener can respond to events bubbling from matching descendants.

quiz.question: Which property refers to the element that triggered the event?
quiz.explanation: event.target is the deepest element involved in the event dispatch.
